skill.name: Microsoft 365 Agents SDK (.NET)
skill.description: Build enterprise agents for Microsoft 365, Teams, and Copilot Studio using the Microsoft.Agents SDK with ASP.NET Core hosting, agent routing, and MSAL-based authentication.
skill.summary: Documentation and code examples for building multichannel agents with Microsoft 365 Agents SDK and ASP.NET Core.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Documentation-only skill with no executable code. Contains C# code examples and configuration templates for Microsoft 365 Agents SDK. No scripts, network operations, file system access, or external commands detected. Static analysis correctly identified this as a prompt-only skill with zero security risk.

content.value_statement: Building multichannel agents for Microsoft 365 requires understanding the Microsoft.Agents SDK hosting model, authentication patterns, and routing architecture. This skill provides production-ready code examples for ASP.NET Core hosting, MSAL authentication, and Copilot Studio integration.
actual_capabilities: Generate ASP.NET Core hosting code for Microsoft.Agents SDK applications
Configure authentication using MSAL with appsettings.json templates
Implement AgentApplication routing for conversation events and message handlers
Create Copilot Studio direct-to-engine clients with token management
Apply best practices for production deployment including storage and security
limitations: Only supports .NET and C# implementations of Microsoft.Agents SDK
Documentation assumes familiarity with ASP.NET Core dependency injection
Configuration examples require Azure AD app registration and tenant setup
Copilot Studio integration requires specific environment credentials

use_cases.title: Create Teams bot with authentication
use_cases.description: Generate a production-ready ASP.NET Core bot that authenticates users via MSAL and responds to messages in Microsoft Teams channels.

use_cases.title: Integrate with Copilot Studio
use_cases.description: Build a console or web application that connects directly to Copilot Studio using the direct-to-engine client pattern with delegated tokens.
use_cases.target_user: Developer extending Copilot Studio with custom agents

use_cases.title: Implement agent error handling
use_cases.description: Add comprehensive error handling and state management to AgentApplication subclasses with proper cleanup and end-of-conversation flows.

prompt_templates.title: Create basic ASP.NET Core agent host
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an ASP.NET Core Minimal API host for a Microsoft.Agents SDK bot. Include AddAgentApplicationOptions, AddAgent for MyAgent class, MemoryStorage, AddAgentAspNetAuthentication, and a POST /api/messages endpoint with IAgentHttpAdapter.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new Microsoft 365 agents project from scratch

prompt_templates.title: Add AgentApplication with routing
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an AgentApplication subclass called SupportAgent with OnConversationUpdate for welcome messages, OnActivity for ActivityTypes.Message routed to a handler method, and OnTurnError for exception handling with state cleanup.
prompt_templates.scenario: Implementing conversation routing logic for a support bot

prompt_templates.title: Configure MSAL authentication handler
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a DelegatingHandler called AddTokenHandler that acquires tokens using MSAL PublicClientApplication with AcquireTokenSilent fallback to AcquireTokenInteractive. Include Bearer header injection and CopilotClient.ScopeFromSettings usage.
prompt_templates.scenario: Implementing token acquisition for Copilot Studio client

prompt_templates.title: Set up Copilot Studio console client
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a HostApplicationBuilder console application that registers CopilotClient with AddHttpClient, custom DelegatingHandler for tokens, and uses StartConversationAsync and AskQuestionAsync to interact with Copilot Studio.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a console app to test Copilot Studio integration

output_examples.input: Create an AgentApplication subclass with a message handler that echoes user input
output_examples.output: Generates a C# class inheriting AgentApplication with constructor registering OnActivity(ActivityTypes.Message, OnMessageAsync), and an OnMessageAsync method that calls turnContext.SendActivityAsync with the echoed text.

output_examples.input: Generate appsettings.json with TokenValidation and ServiceConnection configuration
output_examples.output: Produces a JSON configuration with TokenValidation section containing Enabled, Audiences array, TenantId, and Connections section with AuthType, ClientId, ClientSecret, AuthorityEndpoint, and Scopes for api.botframework.com.

output_examples.input: Show how to add authorization to the /api/messages endpoint in production
output_examples.output: Shows wrapping the POST route with if (!app.Environment.IsDevelopment()) { incomingRoute.RequireAuthorization(); } to enforce authentication outside development environment.

best_practices: Use AgentApplication base classes to centralize routing logic, event handlers, and error handling in one location instead of scattering across middleware.
Replace MemoryStorage with persisted storage like BlobStorage or CosmosDbStorage for production deployments to maintain conversation state across server restarts.
Enable TokenValidation in appsettings.json and apply RequireAuthorization to the /api/messages endpoint to prevent unauthorized access to your agent.
anti_patterns: Storing ClientSecret or other credentials directly in code instead of using configuration providers like Azure Key Vault, environment variables, or managed identity.
Creating new HttpClient instances for each request instead of using IHttpClientFactory to register and reuse HTTP connections efficiently.
Synchronously blocking on async calls by using .Result or .Wait() instead of properly awaiting async SDK methods and passing CancellationToken.

faq.question: What is the difference between AddAgentApplicationOptions and AddAgent?
faq.answer: AddAgentApplicationOptions registers core configuration settings like StartTypingTimer and RemoveRecipientMention. AddAgent registers your specific AgentApplication subclass (like MyAgent) into the dependency injection container.

faq.question: When should I use MemoryStorage versus a persisted storage provider?
faq.answer: Use MemoryStorage only for local development and testing. For production, use BlobStorage, CosmosDbStorage, or another persisted storage provider to maintain conversation state across application restarts and scale-out scenarios.

faq.question: How do I obtain ClientId and ClientSecret for the configuration?
faq.answer: Register an application in Azure Active Directory. The ClientId comes from the app registration overview blade. Create a client secret in the Certificates & secrets section. Use the Directory (tenant) ID for TenantId.

faq.question: What authentication flow should I use for Copilot Studio integration?
faq.answer: For console apps or interactive scenarios, use PublicClientApplication with AcquireTokenInteractive. For server-to-server scenarios like web APIs, use ConfidentialClientApplication with client credentials flow.

faq.question: Why does the /api/messages endpoint check app.Environment.IsDevelopment() before requiring authorization?
faq.answer: This allows local testing without authentication in development mode while enforcing authorization in production. Always test with auth enabled before deploying to ensure your token validation configuration works correctly.

faq.question: Can I host this agent alongside other ASP.NET Core endpoints?
faq.answer: Yes, Microsoft.Agents SDK integrates with standard ASP.NET Core routing. You can add additional controllers, minimal APIs, or middleware alongside the /api/messages endpoint for hybrid applications.
